title: White House nears voluntary 30-day frontier model review deal with OpenAI, Anthropic, Google
subtitle: The framework operationalizing Trump's June 2 executive order is expected before the August 1 deadline; Meta is not part of the pact.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: The White House is finalizing a voluntary framework with OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google that would give federal agencies up to 30 days of pre-release access to new frontier models for national security review. The framework operationalizes Trump's June 2 executive order, which set a 60-day deadline expiring around August 1. Officials on both sides expect an announcement before that date. Meta is not part of the pact. The benchmarks agencies would use to evaluate models are classified.

This is not a licensing regime and not a preclearance requirement — the executive order was explicit that it does not create either. What it does create is a channel: labs agree to hand the government a copy of a covered model early enough for Treasury, Defense, and Homeland Security to run classified cyber and CBRN evaluations before the model reaches customers. The 30 days is a ceiling, not a mandatory wait. Compliance is voluntary but reputational — a lab that refuses to participate is putting itself outside a peer group that has agreed to the review.

The context is the June export-control episode. Anthropic launched Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 on June 9, was forced to pull both after a Commerce Department directive three days later, and only got Fable back online on July 1 after two and a half weeks of negotiation. That episode surfaced the operational problem the new framework is designed to solve: if the government does not see a model before launch, it can only react by cutting it off afterward. A 30-day pre-review lets Washington raise concerns before they turn into shutdown orders. Meta's absence is the counter-signal — the company most publicly committed to open-weight releases is not signing on to a review pipeline that would slow them.

For learners: read the executive order text before reading the coverage. The word that matters is 'voluntary' — it is what distinguishes this from the EU AI Act's mandatory posture and from a hypothetical FDA-style approval regime for models. Understanding which parts of an AI regulatory scheme are mandatory, which are voluntary-with-consequences, and which are pure rhetoric is the single most useful skill for anyone reading policy news right now.
